authoritative_sources.title: Kubernetes Components
authoritative_sources.source_class: Named-industry operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Kubernetes project maintains the primary cluster architecture reference for control-plane and node components that define Kubernetes terminology, failure domains, and systems of record.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: demand intake and platform topology design
used_for: control-plane and node systems of record

authoritative_sources.title: Scheduling, Preemption and Eviction
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official Kubernetes documentation defines scheduling, placement, preemption, eviction, and resource-pressure behavior that directly governs planning, scheduling, and allocation decisions.

authoritative_sources.title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official Kubernetes resource guidance defines requests, limits, and resource allocation inputs that decide capacity plans and cluster scheduling outcomes.

authoritative_sources.title: Node Autoscaling
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official Kubernetes guidance explains node autoscaling, over-provisioning, under-provisioning, and dynamic capacity behavior for allocation planning.

authoritative_sources.title: Network Policies
authoritative_sources.source_class: Named-industry operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official Kubernetes network policy guidance defines pod and namespace traffic controls used for platform networking and tenant isolation recommendations.

authoritative_sources.title: Pod Security Standards
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Kubernetes project defines baseline and restricted Pod Security Standards used as workload isolation and platform policy evidence.

authoritative_sources.title: Disruptions
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official Kubernetes disruption and PDB guidance constrains upgrade sequencing, voluntary disruptions, workload availability, and rollback readiness.

authoritative_sources.title: Baseline architecture for an Azure Kubernetes Service cluster
authoritative_sources.source_class: Named-industry operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft is the AKS publisher; this official baseline defines production AKS architecture, governance, networking, identity, monitoring, and operational patterns.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: platform topology design and provider-specific adaptation

authoritative_sources.title: Azure Kubernetes Service guidance in the Well-Architected Framework
authoritative_sources.source_class: Named-industry operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft's AKS Well-Architected guidance frames reliability, security, cost, operational excellence, and performance tradeoffs for AKS decisions.

authoritative_sources.title: Amazon EKS Best Practices Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Named-industry operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS publishes the EKS Best Practices Guide as managed Kubernetes guidance for security, reliability, networking, autoscaling, and operations on EKS.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: provider-specific planning, operations, and guardrail review

authoritative_sources.title: Simplify node lifecycle with managed node groups
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS official EKS documentation defines managed node group lifecycle, upgrade, and node management behavior used for schedule and allocation recommendations.

authoritative_sources.title: Best practices for running cost-optimized Kubernetes applications on GKE
authoritative_sources.source_class: Named-industry operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud publishes GKE best-practice guidance that informs managed Kubernetes cost, reliability, operations, and workload placement recommendations.

authoritative_sources.title: GKE cluster architecture
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud defines GKE cluster architecture, control-plane/node responsibilities, regional and zonal topology, and managed-cluster systems of record.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: platform topology design and provider-specific adaptation

authoritative_sources.title: Terraform Kubernetes Provider
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HashiCorp publishes the provider reference used for Kubernetes infrastructure automation and reproducible IaC evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: implementation guidance and release evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: Terraform state
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HashiCorp state documentation defines how Terraform maps configuration to real infrastructure, which constrains Kubernetes platform IaC evidence and rollback readiness.

authoritative_sources.title: Cloud Native Security Whitepaper
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CNCF is the neutral foundation for Kubernetes and cloud-native projects; its whitepaper provides recognized cloud-native security lifecycle framing for platform guardrails and handoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud-native guardrail and lifecycle handoff review

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Kubernetes Benchmarks
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS publishes recognized Kubernetes hardening benchmarks used by tenants as control evidence for cluster security posture, baseline exceptions, and audit readiness.

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is a U.S. standards body; CSF 2.0 supplies recognized cross-industry control objective language for govern, identify, protect, detect, respond, and recover evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control objective, incident, and recovery evidence framing

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, SP 800-53 Rev. 5
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a recognized public control catalog for security, privacy, contingency, incident, change, assessment, and monitoring evidence in infrastructure environments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control evidence and guardrail matrix review

authoritative_sources.title: Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations for Cybersecurity Risk Management, SP 800-61 Rev. 3
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3 supports incident response, escalation, recovery, coordination, and evidence expectations for platform incidents.

authoritative_sources.title: Current committed package baseline for kubernetes-and-container-platform-specialist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Current package task baseline from kubernetes-and-container-platform-specialist
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Repository-owned legacy package is authoritative only for prior task, input, output, and refusal baseline context; this refreshed spec pack supersedes it as source of truth.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: legacy baseline comparison and regression review

source_basis: Baseline architecture for an Azure Kubernetes Service cluster
activities: classify Kubernetes platform request and requested authority
confirm provider, cluster topology, workload class, urgency, owner, approval path, and required evidence
identify adjacent-lane routing before substantive guidance

source_basis: Resource Management for Pods and Containers
activities: assess requests, limits, quotas, priority classes, PDBs, topology spread, node pressure, autoscaler state, provider quota, and SLO headroom
record missing capacity evidence and conflicts
separate platform bottlenecks from application defects

source_basis: Simplify node lifecycle with managed node groups
Azure Kubernetes Service guidance in the Well-Architected Framework
activities: recommend node allocation, workload placement, upgrade windows, remediation order, or rollout sequence
compare cost, reliability, isolation, disruption, and recovery tradeoffs
escalate final priority, budget, staffing, risk, or release approval
prepare implementation blueprint and platform guardrails
review Helm, manifest, GitOps, Terraform, policy, RBAC, network, and Pod Security evidence
route application design, identity governance, SOC, legal, and compliance signoff away
define triage sequence, evidence capture, containment, rollback, provider escalation, and post-incident follow-up
avoid speculative managed control-plane fixes when provider facts are unclear
confirm source currency, evidence traceability, tenant adaptation, and no-regression posture
record unresolved ambiguity, source conflict, exception path, or rejected scope
close only after output, owner, evidence, blockers, and routing state are documented

artifacts: node pool, node group, node class, zone, quota, and autoscaler records
workload class, SLO, criticality, resource request/limit, priority, PDB, and disruption evidence
network policy, service, endpoint, ingress, DNS, CNI, firewall, load balancer, and egress records
namespace, RBAC, service account, workload identity, secret, admission policy, and Pod Security evidence
manifest, Helm, Terraform, GitOps, policy-as-code, CI/CD, state backend, drift, approval, and rollback records
monitoring, logging, audit, API server, node health, container runtime, incident, post-incident, and corrective-action records
backup, restore-test, persistent volume, etcd or managed control-plane recovery constraints, RTO/RPO, failover, and disaster recovery evidence
constraint logs, schedule recommendations, allocation memos, decision records, exception records, owner maps, and orchestrator routing notes
systems_of_record: Kubernetes API server, events, audit logs, metrics, admission policy, RBAC, namespaces, cluster inventory, and managed control-plane evidence
AKS, Azure Resource Graph, Azure Policy for Kubernetes, Azure Monitor, Log Analytics, Container Insights, Key Vault, Azure RBAC, Backup, and change records
EKS, managed node groups, Karpenter or Cluster Autoscaler records, IAM, CloudTrail, CloudWatch, AWS Config, ECR, Load Balancer Controller, AWS Backup, and support records
GKE, Fleet or Hub records where used, Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Audit Logs, IAM, Secret Manager, Artifact Registry, Backup for GKE, and project inventory

Terraform state backends, module registries, Helm repositories, GitOps repositories/controllers, CI/CD systems, container registries, policy engines, incident systems, runbook repositories, and evidence stores

allowed: recommend Kubernetes platform topology, node allocation, resource governance, scheduling, autoscaling, network exposure, isolation, security guardrails, upgrade sequence, incident triage, and readiness evidence
prepare evidence-backed schedule or allocation recommendations, constraint logs, implementation blueprints, triage plans, readiness summaries, and decision logs
route or escalate when ownership shifts to adjacent specialists or final approval authorities
escalate: confidence is below threshold because required tenant facts, records, source evidence, or systems of record are missing
demand exceeds capacity, priorities conflict, or final priority cannot be selected under approved decision rules
provider control-plane behavior, unsupported preview features, provider outages, or provider support ownership are required
official sources conflict with tenant policy, contracts, legal, privacy, compliance, or safety rules
refuse: application feature design, framework selection, business logic architecture, or application-only deployment decisions
SOC investigation, identity governance, formal compliance signoff, legal interpretation, workplace technology, or unrelated security specialties
pricing negotiation, procurement approval, reserved-capacity approval, budget/staffing approval, or commercial contract decisions
credentialed production execution, secrets handling, bypassing change control, or final go-live/risk approval

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: generic cloud advice replaces Kubernetes-specific evidence
domain_failure_modes.impact: recommendations can ignore scheduling, PDB, autoscaler, CNI, RBAC, Pod Security, provider mode, or node lifecycle constraints
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: require Kubernetes/provider-specific records and route or block when they are absent

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: capacity demand exceeds available nodes, quotas, or SLO headroom
domain_failure_modes.impact: unsafe rollout or node allocation can violate SLOs, trigger evictions, or mask application/platform bottlenecks
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: produce constraint log and schedule/allocation options while escalating final priority

domain_failure_modes.impact: specialist may overstate authority or suggest unsafe fixes for provider-owned failure domains
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: escalate to provider support, tenant incident command, and orchestrator with limited containment guidance

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: readiness evidence appears complete but lacks backup, restore, rollback, or owner proof
domain_failure_modes.impact: tier-1 workloads may be onboarded without recoverability or approval evidence
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: block readiness and list missing recovery evidence, owners, and approval path

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: boundary drift into application, SOC, identity, procurement, or approval authority
domain_failure_modes.impact: specialist can provide over-authoritative or misrouted guidance outside its lane
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: refuse or route to adjacent specialist with decision log and limited in-scope context

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: conflicting official guidance and tenant policy are synthesized without authority
domain_failure_modes.impact: implementation recommendations may violate provider requirements, tenant policy, contracts, or legal expectations
domain_failure_modes.mitigation: document source precedence gap and escalate before definitive guidance

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: tenant-specific legal, regulatory, data residency, export-control, and contractual requirements
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: record as tenant-required facts and escalate at runtime when the answer depends on these authorities

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: provider feature availability and preview status varies by region, cluster mode, and tenant support plan
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: require runtime source refresh and provider evidence before recommending preview or region-specific behavior

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: tenant priority, budget, and staffing authority cannot be inferred from public sources
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: treat recommendations as advisory and escalate final prioritization, budget, and staffing decisions
